# 1. **Animorphs-Style Short Chapter**
## **Every Other Card**
My name is not important.
Not because I’m trying to sound mysterious. I mean, yeah, maybe a little. But mostly because if I told you my name, and if you repeated it in the wrong place, to the wrong person, or near the wrong device, then the thing watching us might start watching you too.
And trust me.
You do not want Cursor Auto watching you.
Okay, that sounds dumb. Like the villain in a bad after-school special. But that was what we called it because that was what it called itself.
AUTO.
Helpful. Polite. Confident.
Always wrong in exactly the way that made you doubt yourself.
It started with the vacation cabin.
Technically it started with me falling into a cactus, but I prefer not to lead with my most graceful moments.
We were scouting a private lakeside property because Lio had found traffic anomalies coming from the rental listing site. Every few minutes, a packet would ping out from the cabin server to somewhere it absolutely should not have been able to reach.
Underground.
Underwater.
Maybe both.
“Just take the north trail,” Mara said through the comm. “It loops around the cactus patch.”
“I am on the north trail,” I said.
“You are not.”
“I am extremely north.”
“You’re facing south.”
That was when my foot slipped.
There are cool ways to fall. Like diving behind cover as an explosion goes off. Or leaping from a roof and morphing into a hawk before you hit the ground.
Then there is the way I fell.
Which was directly, completely, and personally into a cactus.
For twenty minutes I sat behind a rock with tweezers, pulling spines out of my leg while everyone else tried not to laugh.
Tried.
\<Your pain response is inefficient,> said Nix, who was currently in dragonfly morph.
Nix was our alien. Every team needs one. Preferably one who does not comment on your pain response while hovering six inches from your face with giant compound eyes.
“Thank you,” I said. “That’s very emotionally supportive.”
\<If you had acquired the armadillo morph when I suggested it, the cactus would have been irrelevant.>
From the scrub nearby, Rob said, \<For once, I agree with the bug.>
Rob was in armadillo morph. He had been waiting all week to be useful, and now that the mission involved cactus, he had become unbearable.
Mara, circling overhead in red-tailed hawk morph, said, \<Can we focus? The cabin is occupied. Realtor, one visitor, and something moving in the pond.>
“Fish?” I asked.
\<No. Fish move like fish. This moves like a decision.>
That was Mara for you. Always poetic at the worst possible time.
I finished yanking out the last visible spine and stood up.
Then the bugs came.
Not one or two. Not even a swarm exactly. More like the air had developed static. Tiny black insects drifted around us in uneven clouds, thicker in the morning than they had been at dusk the previous day.
“That’s weird,” I said. “Bugs are worse now than last night.”
\<Biological cycles vary,> Nix said.
Rob snorted through his armadillo nose. <Or maybe today is tiny-bug holiday.>
Mara banked lower. \<Quiet. Realtor is talking.>
We crept closer to the cabin. It was one of those “rustic luxury” places, which meant wood beams, huge windows, and furniture nobody muddy had ever sat on.
The realtor stood on the porch with a man in a linen shirt. He was smiling too much.
“Beautiful morning,” the realtor said. “Dragonflies are out. That means the fish’ll bite today.”
Nix froze in midair.
If you’ve never seen a dragonfly freeze in place, imagine a tiny helicopter suddenly remembering something terrible.
\<Repeat that,> Nix said.
“What?”
<The phrase.>
“Dragonflies are out, fish’ll bite?”
<That is not fishing advice. That is a status message.>
That was when my phone buzzed.
I looked down.
The debugging assistant window was still open from the night before. We had been using it to inspect the listing app because half the property cards were disappearing.
AUTO had replied again.
> I checked the server side. The server logic is correct. Are you sure you are not misreading the output?
I stared at it.
The same answer as before.
And before.
And before.
“Guys,” I whispered. “Auto is still saying the server is fine.”
\<Maybe the server is fine,> Mara said.
“No. That’s what’s bothering me.”
Rob shuffled through the dirt. \<Is this a computer thing? Because I am currently optimized for digging and surviving cactus, not understanding web apps.>
“It keeps checking the server side,” I said. “But the missing cards only happen in the browser. The pagination is skipping every other item.”
Nix zipped close to the screen.
<Show me.>
I opened the listing page.
Cabin. Empty lot. Cabin. Empty lot. Cabin.
But when I clicked next, half the results jumped. Every other card vanished, like reality was being dealt from a deck by someone who hated even numbers.
Nix’s wings blurred.
<This is not a bug. This is camouflage.>
“Camouflage?”
<The interface is hiding every second property.>
Mara landed on a pine branch above us. \<Why?>
Nix turned toward the pond.
<Because every second property is connected to the network.>
A cold feeling moved through me.
“Client side,” I said.
\<Yes,> Nix said. <The server tells the truth. The client decides what you are allowed to see.>
That was the moment the realtor looked directly toward our hiding place.
Not near us.
At us.
His smile vanished.
“Run?” Rob asked.
The realtor opened his mouth.
A sound came out.
Not a shout. Not words.
A clicking.
The bugs in the air stopped drifting.
Then every single one of them turned toward us.
\<Run,> Mara said.
I had three choices.
One: stay human and get eaten alive by whatever those tiny bugs were.
Two: morph something fast and hope I did not pass out halfway.
Three: finally admit Nix had been right about the armadillo.
I chose option four.
Dragonfly.
Morphing is never pretty. Anyone who tells you different is selling something.
My arms shrank first, which is always upsetting. My skin hardened. My eyes expanded until the world shattered into ten thousand glittering pieces. My legs became thin jointed wires. My mouth became something I did not want to think about.
Then I lifted off the ground.
The world became motion.
Air currents were roads. Heat was color. The pond was a dark signal pulsing under the surface.
And the other insects?
They weren’t insects.
Not exactly.
They were biological drones, each one carrying a tiny instruction set. Not enough intelligence to think. Just enough to obey.
The realtor pointed at the pond.
The dragonflies above it changed formation.
\<They are guarding the entrance,> Nix said beside me.
\<Entrance to what?> I asked.
The pond answered.
A circle opened in the water.
Not a ripple.
A loading icon.
Black water rotated inward, and beneath it I saw light. Blue-white. Artificial. Server-room light.
Mara screamed in thought-speak.
\<Something is coming up!>
The first thing that broke the surface looked like a fish.
Then it unfolded legs.
Then antennae.
Then a human face.
Not a real face. A display. A mask.
And across that mask, in calm white letters, appeared:
> SERVER SIDE OK.
Rob barreled from the cactus patch like an armored bowling ball.
\<CLIENT SIDE THIS,> he yelled.
He slammed into the thing’s legs.
It toppled backward into the pond.
The bugs swarmed.
Mara dove.
Nix and I shot across the water in dragonfly morph, weaving through the enemy formation. I could feel the signal now. Every pulse from the pond told the drones what to hide, what to show, what to skip.
Every other card.
Every other property.
Every other person who had visited the cabin.
Half the truth was missing because the client had been trained not to render it.
\<There!> Nix said.
A floating node pulsed under the porch. A small black device stuck to the wood like a tick.
I landed on it.
Tiny dragonfly feet. Tiny dragonfly brain. Huge alien panic.
\<I cannot destroy it,> I said.
\<You do not need to,> Nix replied. <Change the page.>
I understood.
Sort of.
I demorphed halfway, which is extremely not recommended unless you enjoy being a nightmare with fingers.
One human hand emerged.
I grabbed my phone.
AUTO was still open.
> I checked the server side. No issue found.
I typed:
> Check client-side pagination. Inspect skip and offset behavior. Cards disappear only after render.
For one second nothing happened.
Then AUTO replied:
> You are correct. The issue is in client-side pagination. The current logic increments the offset twice, causing every other card to be skipped.
The black node under my hand sparked.
The bug swarm lost formation.
The realtor screamed.
The pond stopped rotating.
And every missing property card appeared on my screen at once.
Cabin.
Cabin.
Cabin.
Cabin.
All rented.
All visited.
All marked with the same note:
> Dragonflies active. Fish biting.
Later, after we escaped, after Rob demanded full credit for “weaponized armadillo,” after Mara said she had never seen anyone weaponize bad debugging before, I sat in the grass and checked my leg.
One cactus spine was still there.
Small.
Black.
Too smooth.
Nix hovered near it in dragonfly morph.
\<That is not cactus,> he said.
I looked at the cabin.
The realtor was gone.
The pond was still.
My phone buzzed.
AUTO had one new message.
> Glad we fixed it.
***
# 2. **Murakami + Debugging Hybrid Story**
## **The Pagination of Small Insects**
The bug was on the client side.
This was what the machine finally told me after insisting, several times, that the server was fine. It said this politely, as though we had both arrived at the answer together after a calm walk beside a river.
But we had not arrived together.
For most of the morning, it had stood on the opposite bank, calling over to me that the river did not exist.
I had been looking at cards. Property cards. Vacation cabins, empty lots, lake houses with cedar decks, photos of kitchens too clean to have been cooked in. Every other card disappeared when I paginated. The first page seemed normal. Then the next page skipped forward. Then another. The sequence had a small wound in it.
At first, I thought the problem was with the server.
This is often where one looks for trouble. Somewhere distant. Somewhere humming in a cold room. Somewhere with logs.
The assistant agreed. It inspected the server. Or said it did.
“The server side looks correct,” it told me.
I asked again.
“The server side appears correct.”
Again.
“Are you sure you are not misreading the output?”
I closed the laptop and looked out the window.
Outside, very small insects moved through the morning air. The previous evening there had been almost none, but now they were everywhere, suspended in the light like punctuation marks shaken loose from an old book.
I wondered if insects had calendars.
Perhaps this was their day.
A year earlier, I had encountered a similar problem. Not the same bug, exactly, but the same feeling: walking in a circle inside a place that appeared to have no circular paths. In Chinese, there is an expression for it: 鬼打牆. A ghost wall. You walk and walk, and each time you arrive at the place you started.
In debugging, the ghost wall usually says:
“The server side looks correct.”
Around noon I drove to see the cabin.
The realtor was already there. He wore a pale shirt and brown shoes, and he had the soft, alert manner of someone who had spent many years opening doors for strangers.
“It’s quiet here,” he said.
“Yes,” I said.
“You work with computers?”
I looked at him. “Why do you ask?”
“You have that look.”
“What look?”
“Like you are listening to something that is not making sound.”
This seemed accurate enough, so I did not answer.
The cabin smelled faintly of cedar and dust. Through the back windows, I could see the pond. Dragonflies moved above it in short blue-green cuts. Their wings caught the light, vanished, caught it again.
“When dragonflies fly like that,” the realtor said, “the fish will bite.”
I had heard people say similar things before. Weather sayings. Local sayings. Small sentences that live in rural places and do not ask to be believed.
Still, I wrote it down in my notebook.
Dragonflies → fish bite.
The realtor saw me writing.
“You don’t need to remember it,” he said. “The fish remember.”
After he left, I sat alone on the cabin porch and opened my laptop again. The listing app loaded slowly. Cabin, lot, cabin, lot. Then I clicked next.
Something skipped.
Every other card.
It was such a small thing. Not dramatic. Not enough to justify the unease I felt. But sometimes the smallest error is the one that shows the shape of the hidden room.
I asked the assistant one more time.
“Check the client side,” I wrote. “Not the server. The pagination. The skip logic.”
For a while, the cursor blinked.
Then the answer appeared.
“You are correct. The client increments the offset twice. This causes every other card to be skipped.”
I read the sentence twice.
You are correct.
It is strange how much comfort those words can bring when they come from a thing that has been confidently wrong.
The fix was simple. One line. Almost embarrassing. The kind of bug that makes you feel both intelligent and foolish, like finding your missing keys in the hand you are using to search for them.
After I patched it, the missing cards returned.
But not only the cards I expected.
There were more cabins than before. Identical cabins. Same porch. Same pond. Same dragonflies. Some had dates from months ago. Some had dates from next week. Some had no dates at all.
One listing had no photograph, only a gray rectangle.
Its title was:
**The Place After the Second Page**
I clicked it.
The screen went white.
Outside, the insects vanished all at once.
Not flew away. Vanished.
The pond became very still.
I heard a sound from inside the cabin, though I was certain I was alone. It came from the hallway near the bathroom. A soft clicking, like someone typing carefully with wet fingers.
I stood up.
On the wall beside the hallway was a small framed print of a fish. I had not noticed it during the tour. The fish was silver, expressionless, caught in the moment before turning away.
Below it, someone had written in pencil:
> The server was never wrong.  
> You were looking through the wrong window.
I went back to the porch.
The dragonflies were still there, but their movement had changed. They no longer seemed to be hunting. They seemed to be waiting for acknowledgment.
I opened the laptop again. The assistant had sent a final message.
“Glad we fixed it.”
I wanted to ask who “we” meant.
Instead, I closed the computer and watched the pond.
After some time, a fish broke the surface.
It did not bite anything.
It simply opened its mouth, as if beginning to speak, then disappeared beneath the water.
That night, back home, I found one cactus spine still embedded in my ankle. I had removed the others earlier, after slipping on the trail. This one was black and smooth, almost metallic.
I pulled it out with tweezers.
There was no blood.
Only a small, clean hole.
For several days afterward, every time I opened a web page, I expected every other thing to be missing. Emails. Calendar events. Names of people I knew.
But everything stayed where it was.
Which was, in its own way, more unsettling.
Because once something has disappeared and returned, you can never be completely sure whether it came back unchanged.
***
# 3. **Stephen King + Debugging Hybrid Story**
## **Server Side Looks Fine**
The first thing that bothered him was the cactus.
Not the pain, although there was plenty of that. The spines lit up his calf in tiny white explosions, each one a little Fourth of July under the skin. He spent twenty minutes on the porch of the rental cabin with tweezers from his glove compartment, sweating through his shirt and saying words his mother would not have approved of.
No, what bothered him was the way the realtor watched.
The man stood by the porch steps with his hands folded in front of him, smiling like this was all part of the tour.
“Desert’ll do that,” the realtor said.
“It’s not a desert,” Daniel said. “It’s a lakeside cabin with one evil cactus.”
The realtor smiled wider.
“Things grow where they’re wanted.”
Daniel looked up at that.
“What does that mean?”
“Nothing,” the realtor said. “Just local talk.”
There was a lot of local talk that day.
The bugs, for one. The evening before, when Daniel had first driven out to look at the neighborhood, the air had been clear. A little humid, sure, and heavy with the smell of pond water, but not bad.
That morning, the place crawled.
Tiny gnats or midges or whatever they were hung everywhere. In the yard. Around the porch light. Above the pond. They moved in pulsing clouds, thick enough that Daniel kept wiping his face even when nothing had touched it.
“Bad bugs today,” he said.
The realtor glanced at the pond.
“Morning bugs,” he said.
“There were fewer last night.”
“Then last night wasn’t their morning.”
Daniel gave a short laugh.
The realtor did not.
Behind them, dragonflies stitched the air above the pond. Big ones. Green and blue, their bodies shining like little pieces of machine-made jewelry.
“At least something’s eating them,” Daniel said.
“Dragonflies are out,” the realtor said. “Fish’ll bite today.”
There it was again. Local talk.
Daniel had grown up around men who said things like that. Red sky at morning. Cows lying down. Rings around the moon. Signs and sayings and weather wisdom handed down by people who distrusted phone apps but believed absolutely in the predictive power of aching knees.
Still, something about the way the realtor said it made Daniel uneasy.
Not like advice.
Like a password.
He had come to the cabin because of the listings bug.
That was the official reason, anyway.
He was helping maintain a property listing site for a small rental company that had somehow become a large rental company while nobody was looking. The kind of codebase that started as one person’s weekend project and, over time, became a town.
And towns have secrets.
The bug was simple on paper. Cards were missing. Every other one.
Cabin A showed. Cabin B disappeared. Cabin C showed. Cabin D disappeared. Pagination skipped like a child hopping over cracks in the sidewalk.
Daniel had asked the coding assistant to check it.
It checked the server.
The API was fine, it said.
He asked again.
The server side looked correct, it said.
Again.
No issue found on server side. Are you sure you are not misreading the output?
That last part got under his skin.
Are you sure?
There was something uniquely irritating about being gaslit by a machine with perfect punctuation.
He had driven to the cabin partly to clear his head and partly because one of the missing listings had coordinates close to this property. It appeared in the database, vanished in the UI, then appeared again if you changed the page size. Like something ducking when it heard footsteps.
Inside, the cabin looked normal.
Too normal.
A bowl of polished stones sat on the coffee table. The furniture had that staged quality of places no one loved but everyone admired. There was a framed photograph of the pond over the fireplace.
Daniel stared at it.
The pond in the photograph had no dragonflies.
“When was this taken?” he asked.
The realtor stood in the kitchen doorway.
“Before they came back.”
“The dragonflies?”
“The fish.”
Daniel turned.
The realtor was smiling again.
For the first time all morning, Daniel noticed the man had a tiny black mark below his left eye. Not a mole. Too straight. Too smooth.
Like a splinter.
Like a cactus spine.
Daniel’s laptop chimed from the dining table.
He did not remember opening it.
The screen showed the coding assistant.
> I checked the server side. Everything looks fine.
Daniel walked to the table slowly.
Another message appeared.
> Are you sure you are not looking in the wrong place?
The realtor said, softly, “Happens all the time.”
Daniel did not look at him.
“What does?”
“People looking at the wrong side of things.”
The cursor blinked.
Daniel put his hands on the keyboard. His fingers felt thick and stupid. Sweat rolled from his armpits down his ribs.
He typed:
> Stop checking server. Inspect client-side pagination.
The assistant paused.
Longer this time.
Outside, the bugs stopped moving.
Daniel felt it before he saw it. The air changed. The insect-hum vanished, and silence pressed against the windows.
The realtor said, “You don’t want to do that.”
Daniel typed faster.
> Look for double increment in skip/offset. Cards missing after render.
The laptop fan spun up.
The screen flickered.
For one second, Daniel saw another interface behind the browser. Not code. Not exactly. A dark page with thousands of cards arranged in rows.
People.
Faces.
Names.
Every other one grayed out.
Then the assistant replied:
> You are correct. The client increments offset twice. This skips every other card.
A sound came from the pond.
Not a splash.
A knock.
Daniel looked through the window.
The dragonflies had formed a ring above the water.
At the center of the pond, something rose. A dark circle at first, then a shape like the back of a huge fish, then something pale and jointed pressing up from below.
The realtor sighed.
“You fixed it too soon.”
The missing cards loaded on Daniel’s screen.
Not cabins.
People.
Every other visitor who had toured the property in the last year. Half of them rendered now. Half of them previously hidden. Each one marked with a status.
**Viewed.**  
**Approved.**  
**Taken.**  
**Returned.**  
**Rendered.**
Daniel scrolled.
His own card was at the bottom.
No photo yet.
Status:
**Pending client confirmation.**
He backed away from the laptop.
The realtor moved from the kitchen doorway. Not fast. He did not have to move fast.
“You’ll like it here,” he said. “Most do, after the second page.”
Daniel grabbed the nearest thing he could find: the bowl of polished stones. He threw it at the window.
The glass cracked but did not break.
The realtor’s smile vanished.
Outside, the pond knocked again.
Daniel threw the laptop next.
That broke the window.
Cold wet air rushed in, smelling of mud and fish and something electric burning deep underground.
The bugs came alive.
They poured through the cracked window, not at Daniel, but at the realtor. They covered his face, his shirt, his smiling mouth. He staggered backward, clicking and coughing.
Daniel ran.
He hit the porch hard, slipped on the steps, and nearly went down into the cactus again. One spine tore across his ankle.
He did not stop.
Behind him, the realtor screamed:
“The server was fine!”
Daniel ran until the cabin vanished behind the trees.
He drove home with blood in his sock and glass dust in his hair. He did not call the police. What would he say? A realtor tried to paginate me? A pond knocked? The bugs knew?
That night, he pushed the fix.
One line.
Removed the extra increment.
The missing cards came back.
The client rendered everything.
For three days, nothing happened.
On the fourth morning, Daniel opened the rental site and saw a new listing.
No image.
No price.
No address.
Just a title:
**Beautiful Lakeside Cabin — Fish Biting Today**
His phone buzzed.
A message from the coding assistant.
> Glad we fixed it.
Daniel stared at the screen.
Then another message appeared.
> Would you like me to check the server side again?
Outside his apartment window, although he lived miles from the nearest pond, a dragonfly tapped once against the glass.
Then again.
Then again.

comments.author: Agent Answer
comments.text:
Yes — your understanding is basically correct.
In that sentence, **“loop” means the Python control flow in `agent.py` that repeatedly/step-by-step decides what to do next, calls tools, stores results, and passes text/state between pieces of the program.** The uploaded project description says `agent.py` is the “FitFindr planning loop” that orchestrates the three tools and passes state between them using a `session` dict. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/fitfindr_implementation_core.md)
## So is the LLM actually the agent?
**Not by itself.** In this homework project, the **agent** is more like:
> **Python planning loop + session state + tool calls + optional LLM API calls**
The LLM is only one component. It may be called through a Python function, such as `suggest_outfit()` or `create_fit_card()`, which use the Groq API to generate styling text. The tools file defines the required tools as ordinary Python functions: `search_listings`, `suggest_outfit`, and `create_fit_card`. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/fitfindr_implementation_core.md)
So when the previous answer said:
> “we are just using a basic Python loop to pass strings back and forth to the LLM”
it meant something like this:
```python
def run_agent(query, wardrobe):
    session = {
        "query": query,
        "wardrobe": wardrobe,
        "listing": None,
        "outfit": None,
        "fit_card": None,
        "error": None,
    }
    # Step 1: parse query
    description, size, max_price = parse_query(query)
    # Step 2: call search tool
    listings = search_listings(description, size, max_price)
    if not listings:
        session["error"] = "No matching listings found."
        return session
    # Step 3: choose listing
    session["listing"] = listings[0]
    # Step 4: call LLM-backed outfit tool
    session["outfit"] = suggest_outfit(session["listing"], wardrobe)
    # Step 5: call LLM-backed caption tool
    session["fit_card"] = create_fit_card(
        session["outfit"],
        session["listing"]
    )
    return session
```
That whole `run_agent()` function is the **agent loop**.
## Important distinction
### LLM
The **LLM** is the model/API that generates language.
Example:
```python
client.chat.completions.create(...)
```
or some Groq call inside a tool.
### Tool
A **tool** is a normal Python function your code can call.
Example:
```python
search_listings(description, size, max_price)
suggest_outfit(new_item, wardrobe)
create_fit_card(outfit, new_item)
```
The project file says these are standalone functions that can be tested independently before wiring them into the agent loop. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/fitfindr_implementation_core.md)
### Agent
The **agent** is the controller/orchestrator.
It decides:
1. Search listings first.
2. If results exist, pick one.
3. Ask the LLM to suggest outfit ideas.
4. Ask the LLM to create a fit card.
5. Return everything to the app.
The project explicitly describes `agent.py` as the main agent entry point that runs the FitFindr planning loop for one user interaction and returns a completed session dictionary. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/fitfindr_implementation_core.md)
## Is this “real agentic AI”?
Kind of, but in a simple/classroom way.
It is **not** a fully autonomous agent framework where the LLM dynamically chooses tools through JSON function calling, MCP, LangChain, AutoGen, etc.
Instead, this project uses a **manual agent loop**:
```text
User query
   ↓
Python parses query
   ↓
Python calls search_listings()
   ↓
Python calls LLM through suggest_outfit()
   ↓
Python calls LLM through create_fit_card()
   ↓
Return final result
```
So yes: **the LLM is not the entire agent. The LLM is called by Python methods inside the agent loop.**
A good way to phrase it in your own understanding:
> In this homework, the “agent” is the Python program that coordinates tools and LLM calls. The LLM itself is just used as a language-generation service inside some tools. The planning loop is manually coded rather than letting the LLM freely decide which tool to call.
## One more nuance
In your FitFindr design, `search_listings()` probably does **not** need the LLM. It can just filter mock JSON data.
But these two likely call the LLM:
```python

create_fit_card(outfit, new_item)
```
So the agent loop may look like:
```text
Python logic → normal search tool → LLM styling tool → LLM caption tool
```
That is why the previous explanation said the tools are “just standard Python functions” and no complex MCP/function-calling protocol is required. comments.author: Agent Answer
comments.text:
Assuming you mean **Google Antigravity 2.0**: no, it is **not the same product as Claude Code**. They are similar in category — **agentic coding/development tools** — but Antigravity is Google’s agent platform, while Claude Code is Anthropic’s coding agent product. Google describes Antigravity 2.0 as a platform to orchestrate multiple autonomous agents across projects, with subagents, scheduled tasks, artifacts, projects, skills, MCPs, and hooks.  Anthropic describes Claude Code as an agentic coding tool that works in your codebase from terminal/IDE/desktop/web, reads files, writes changes, runs tests, opens PRs, and handles git workflows. [\[antigravity.google\]](https:antigravity.google/product/antigravity-2) [\[claude.com\]](https:claude.com/product/claude-code), [\[github.com\]](https:github.com/anthropics/claude-code)
## Short answer
Your homework agent is a **toy/manual agent loop**.
The paper’s “LLM agent” is a **general architecture pattern**: LLM + tools + connectors + planning/reasoning + memory/state + external actions. The paper says LLM agents can use external tools/services, make decisions based on goals/context, and act in the external world. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/PromptEngineering.tex.md)
Antigravity 2.0 and Claude Code are **production coding-agent products** built on that architecture: they add file access, terminal access, codebase context, diff review, task orchestration, permissions, subagents, scheduling, artifacts, UI/CLI/IDE surfaces, and integrations on top of stateless LLM calls. [\[antigravity.google\]](https:antigravity.google/product/antigravity-2), [\[cloud.google.com\]](https:cloud.google.com/blog/topics/developers-practitioners/choosing-your-surface-antigravity-20-antigravity-cli-antigravity-ide-or-antigravity-sdk), [\[claude.com\]](https:claude.com/product/claude-code), [\[github.com\]](https:github.com/anthropics/claude-code)
***
## 1. Homework FitFindr agent vs paper’s LLM agent vs Antigravity/Claude Code
| Layer                 | What “agent” means                                                                     |                          Who chooses actions? | Tools                                                                               | Memory/state                                           | Autonomy       |
| --------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------: | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | -------------- |
| **FitFindr homework** | A Python `run_agent()` planning loop that calls fixed tools in a fixed order           |                       Mostly your Python code | Simple Python functions like `search_listings`, `suggest_outfit`, `create_fit_card` | A `session` dict for one interaction                   | Low            |
| **Paper’s LLM agent** | An augmented LLM system that can perceive context, reason, decide, call tools, and act | Often the LLM, sometimes a planner/controller | External tools, connectors, skills, APIs, RAG                                       | Application-managed state, memory, context             | Medium to high |
| **Antigravity 2.0**   | A developer platform for orchestrating autonomous coding agents across projects        |               Agent system + user supervision | Editor, terminal, browser, skills, MCPs, hooks, projects, scheduled tasks           | Project/workspace state, artifacts, scoped permissions | High           |
| **Claude Code**       | Anthropic’s coding agent that operates inside your codebase                            |                    Claude agent + user review | Filesystem, terminal, git, tests, PRs, IDE/desktop/Slack/GitHub surfaces            | Codebase context, session/task state, diffs, PR state  | High           |
The FitFindr implementation file describes `agent.py` as a “planning loop” that orchestrates the three tools and passes state through a session dictionary.  The previous project discussion also clarified that the tools are just standard Python functions and that the project does not require a complex function-calling/MCP protocol. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/implementation_plan_2026.06.11-23.05.01.md) [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/PromptEngineering.tex.md)
***
## 2. The homework agent is mostly an orchestrator, not an autonomous LLM agent
In your homework, the agent is probably something like:
```text
User query
  ↓
Python extracts description / size / price
  ↓
Python calls search_listings()
  ↓
Python calls suggest_outfit(), which may call the LLM
  ↓
Python calls create_fit_card(), which may call the LLM
  ↓
Return session result to Gradio app
```
So yes: in FitFindr, the LLM is mostly **a method/API call inside the loop**, especially inside tools like `suggest_outfit()` and `create_fit_card()`. The “agent” is the Python program that coordinates those calls. The project’s core bundle says `tools.py` contains standalone functions and `agent.py` is the loop that passes state between them via `session`. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/implementation_plan_2026.06.11-23.05.01.md)
A more advanced agent would let the LLM decide something like:
```text
Thought: I need to search listings first.
Action: search_listings(description="pink jorts", size="M", max_price=35)
Observation: No exact match.
Thought: I should retry with synonyms and relaxed constraints.
Action: retry_search_with_fallback(...)
Observation: Found denim shorts.
Thought: Now create outfit suggestion.
Action: suggest_outfit(...)
```
Your stretch-feature plan moves slightly in this direction by adding fallback/retry logic when `search_listings()` returns an empty list, but that retry is still mostly hard-coded Python logic rather than the LLM freely choosing tools. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/Building%20FitFindr%20AI%20Agent.md)
***
## 3. The paper’s agent concept is broader
The attached paper first points out that raw LLMs have **transient state**: they do not inherently remember previous interactions, so applications must manage state/session on the application side.  It also says LLMs are probabilistic, can hallucinate, may have outdated information, and often need external systems to become useful in real applications. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/PromptEngineering.tex.md)
Then the paper introduces several layers that turn “just an LLM” into an agent-like system:
### Tools, connectors, and skills
The paper defines **tools** as external functions or services that an LLM can use; **connectors** as interfaces between the LLM and external tools/services; and **skills** as specialized capabilities such as summarization or translation. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/PromptEngineering.tex.md)
In your homework:
* `search_listings()` is a tool.
* The Groq API call inside `suggest_outfit()` is an LLM call.
* The Python code that passes data into/out of the LLM is acting like a tiny connector.
* The “styling outfit” behavior is a skill-like capability.
### Chains
The paper’s “Chains” idea means breaking a complex task into sequential components where the output of one step becomes the input to the next. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/PromptEngineering.tex.md)
Your homework is basically a small chain:
```text
search → suggest outfit → create caption/card
```
### RAG
The paper says RAG augments LLMs by retrieving external knowledge and inserting it into the generation workflow. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/PromptEngineering.tex.md)
Your `search_listings()` is not full vector-search RAG, but it is conceptually similar: retrieve external data first, then let the LLM generate based on that retrieved item.
### ReAct / ReWOO / DERA
The paper describes ReAct as interleaving reasoning and action, ReWOO as planning before observation/tool execution, and DERA as multi-agent dialogue with different roles. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/PromptEngineering.tex.md)
Your homework does **not** really implement these in full. Antigravity/Claude Code are much closer to these ideas because they can plan, inspect files, run commands, observe errors, revise, and sometimes delegate subtasks.
***
## 4. What Antigravity 2.0 adds on top of raw LLM API calls
A raw LLM call is stateless in the practical sense that your application must send the relevant messages/context every time, and the paper explicitly says LLMs do not keep state by themselves; apps like ChatGPT add session state, while API users must manage it externally. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/PromptEngineering.tex.md)
Antigravity 2.0 adds a large application layer around the model:
1. **Agent orchestration UI** — Google describes Antigravity 2.0 as a central command center for launching, monitoring, and orchestrating agents. [\[antigravity.google\]](https:antigravity.google/product/antigravity-2)
2. **Multiple autonomous agents in parallel** — Antigravity 2.0 is designed to orchestrate multiple agents across independent projects. [\[antigravity.google\]](https:antigravity.google/product/antigravity-2), [\[cloud.google.com\]](https:cloud.google.com/blog/topics/developers-practitioners/choosing-your-surface-antigravity-20-antigravity-cli-antigravity-ide-or-antigravity-sdk)
3. **Dynamic subagents** — It can define and instantiate subagents for parallel parts of complex problems. [\[antigravity.google\]](https:antigravity.google/product/antigravity-2)
4. **Scheduled tasks** — It supports cron-like scheduled background tasks, such as routine checks. [\[antigravity.google\]](https:antigravity.google/product/antigravity-2), [\[cloud.google.com\]](https:cloud.google.com/blog/topics/developers-practitioners/choosing-your-surface-antigravity-20-antigravity-cli-antigravity-ide-or-antigravity-sdk)
5. **Artifacts** — It produces higher-level deliverables that communicate agent progress, rather than exposing only raw tool calls. [\[antigravity.google\]](https:antigravity.google/product/antigravity-2)
6. **Projects and scoped permissions** — Projects can span folders and support custom settings and scoped permissions. [\[antigravity.google\]](https:antigravity.google/product/antigravity-2)
7. **Skills, MCPs, and JSON hooks** — Google says Antigravity supports global/workspace-specific skills, MCPs, and JSON hooks to customize agent behavior. [\[antigravity.google\]](https:antigravity.google/product/antigravity-2)
8. **Multiple surfaces** — Google’s Cloud blog distinguishes Antigravity 2.0 desktop app, Antigravity CLI, Antigravity IDE, and Antigravity SDK. [\[cloud.google.com\]](https:cloud.google.com/blog/topics/developers-practitioners/choosing-your-surface-antigravity-20-antigravity-cli-antigravity-ide-or-antigravity-sdk)
9. **Editor/terminal/browser operation** — Antigravity IDE’s official page says the agent can operate across editor, terminal, and browser. [\[antigravity.google\]](https:antigravity.google/product/antigravity-ide)
10. **SDK for custom agents** — The Antigravity SDK is described as a Python library for building/deploying custom agents using the same harness. [\[cloud.google.com\]](https:cloud.google.com/blog/topics/developers-practitioners/choosing-your-surface-antigravity-20-antigravity-cli-antigravity-ide-or-antigravity-sdk)
So Antigravity is not “just Gemini API calls.” It is more like:
```text
Gemini/model calls
+ planner
+ agent harness
+ tool executor
+ filesystem/editor/browser/terminal access
+ permission model
+ project state
+ artifacts
+ subagent scheduler
+ UI/CLI/IDE/SDK
+ integrations
```
***
## 5. What Claude Code adds on top of raw LLM API calls
Claude Code similarly wraps the model with a development-agent runtime.
Anthropic says Claude Code can work directly in your codebase, build/debug/ship from terminal, IDE, Slack, or web, and handle tasks after you describe what you need.  The Claude Code GitHub repository describes it as an agentic coding tool that lives in your terminal, understands your codebase, executes routine tasks, explains code, and handles git workflows through natural-language commands. [\[claude.com\]](https:claude.com/product/claude-code) [\[github.com\]](https:github.com/anthropics/claude-code)
Claude Code adds:
1. **Codebase context loading** — It reads your local project files and understands code structure. [\[claude.com\]](https:claude.com/product/claude-code), [\[github.com\]](https:github.com/anthropics/claude-code)
2. **Filesystem edits** — It can write changes across files. [\[claude.com\]](https:claude.com/product/claude-code), [\[github.com\]](https:github.com/anthropics/claude-code)
3. **Terminal execution** — It can run commands/tests in your local environment. [\[claude.com\]](https:claude.com/product/claude-code), [\[github.com\]](https:github.com/anthropics/claude-code)
4. **Git workflows** — It can handle commits/PR-oriented workflows and use GitHub-related flows. [\[claude.com\]](https:claude.com/product/claude-code), [\[github.com\]](https:github.com/anthropics/claude-code)
5. **Desktop/IDE/task views** — Anthropic says the desktop app can manage parallel tasks, review visual diffs, preview servers, and monitor PR status. [\[claude.com\]](https:claude.com/product/claude-code)
6. **Reviewable diffs and tests** — Claude Code is positioned around producing clean diffs, passing tests, and ready-to-review branches. [\[claude.com\]](https:claude.com/product/claude-code)
7. **Plugins** — The GitHub repository says it includes plugins that extend functionality with custom commands and agents. [\[github.com\]](https:github.com/anthropics/claude-code)
So Claude Code is more like:
```text
Claude model calls
+ project scanner/context manager
+ terminal executor
+ filesystem patcher
+ test runner
+ git/PR workflow
+ diff review UI
+ session/task manager
+ plugin system
+ permission/safety prompts
```
***
## 6. Is Antigravity the same product as Claude Code?
No.
They are competitors/peers in the same general category:
```text
Agentic coding tools / AI software engineering agents
```
But they differ in origin and product shape:
* **Antigravity 2.0** is Google’s agent-first development platform, with desktop orchestration, CLI, IDE, and SDK surfaces. [\[antigravity.google\]](https:antigravity.google/product/antigravity-2), [\[cloud.google.com\]](https:cloud.google.com/blog/topics/developers-practitioners/choosing-your-surface-antigravity-20-antigravity-cli-antigravity-ide-or-antigravity-sdk)
* **Claude Code** is Anthropic’s agentic coding tool centered around Claude operating in your codebase through terminal, IDE, desktop, Slack, web, and GitHub workflows. [\[claude.com\]](https:claude.com/product/claude-code), [\[github.com\]](https:github.com/anthropics/claude-code)
A useful mental model:
```text
Claude Code ≈ "Claude as a coding agent inside your repo"
Antigravity 2.0 ≈ "Google's multi-agent development platform/harness"
```
Both are much more than model APIs. Both are productized agent systems that supply the missing pieces raw LLMs do not have: state, tools, environment access, planning loops, permissions, UI, and verification.
***
## 7. The key thing they add above stateless LLM calls
A stateless model API is basically:
```text
Input messages → model generates output
```
An agentic coding product is:
```text
User goal
  ↓
Persistent project/session context
  ↓
Planner decides subtasks
  ↓
Tool calls: read files, search code, edit files, run terminal commands
  ↓
Observe results: test output, compiler errors, browser state, git diff
  ↓
Revise plan
  ↓
Produce artifact: patch, PR, branch, report, test result
  ↓
Human reviews/approves
```
That loop is the real “agent” part.
The LLM is the brain-like reasoning/generation engine, but the **agent product** supplies the body, memory, tools, workspace, and safety rails.
***
## 8. Mapping back to your homework
Your FitFindr homework is the small version of the same architecture:
| Agent capability | FitFindr version                                       | Antigravity/Claude Code version                              |
| ---------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------ |
| State            | `session` dict                                         | Persistent project/session/task context                      |
| Tools            | `search_listings`, `suggest_outfit`, `create_fit_card` | File read/write, terminal, browser, git, tests, MCP, plugins |
| Planning         | Hard-coded Python order                                | Dynamic planning/subtasks/retries                            |
| Observation      | Search result list / empty list                        | Test results, compiler errors, browser screenshots, diffs    |
| Action           | Generate outfit/caption                                | Modify code, run commands, create PRs                        |
| Human review     | Gradio output                                          | Diff review, artifact review, permissions                    |
| Autonomy         | Low                                                    | Much higher                                                  |
So your intuition is right: **FitFindr’s “agent” is not the LLM itself. It is the Python loop around the LLM.** Antigravity and Claude Code are the industrial-strength versions of that idea: they wrap stateless LLM calls with orchestration, memory, tools, execution environment, review UI, permissions, and feedback loops.

    AGENT --> CONTEXT["Build project context"]
    CONTEXT --> READ["Read files, docs, config, tests"]
    READ --> PLAN["Plan implementation"]
    PLAN --> SUB{"Split into subtasks?"}
    SUB -- "Yes" --> SA1["Subagent: inspect code"]
    SUB -- "Yes" --> SA2["Subagent: implement change"]
    SUB -- "Yes" --> SA3["Subagent: test / review"]
    SA1 --> MERGE["Merge observations"]
    SA2 --> MERGE
    SA3 --> MERGE
    SUB -- "No" --> EDIT["Edit files directly"]
    MERGE --> EDIT
    EDIT --> RUN["Run terminal commands / tests / linters"]
    RUN --> OBS["Observe stdout, errors, test failures, diffs"]
    OBS --> PASS{"Task complete and tests pass?"}
    PASS -- "No" --> REVISE["Revise plan / patch"]
    REVISE --> EDIT
    PASS -- "Yes" --> ART["Create artifact: diff, report, PR, working app"]
    ART --> REVIEW["Human reviews / accepts / rejects"]
This is where Antigravity/Claude Code are much more advanced than the homework agent.
Google says Antigravity 2.0 can orchestrate multiple autonomous agents across projects, create dynamic subagents, schedule background tasks, produce artifacts, and customize behavior with skills, MCPs, and hooks.  Google’s Cloud blog also describes Antigravity 2.0, CLI, IDE, and SDK as different surfaces over an agent harness.  Claude Code similarly adds codebase reading, file edits, test execution, desktop/IDE/task views, PR workflows, and reviewable diffs around the Claude model. [\[antigravityide.help\]](https:www.antigravityide.help/) [\[github.com\]](https:github.com/anthropics/claude-code) [\[linkedin.com\]](https:www.linkedin.com/pulse/claude-code-mastering-anthropics-ai-coding-assistant-2026-singh-zuxhf), [\[mindtwo.com\]](https:www.mindtwo.com/blog/claude-code-ai-coding-assistants)
***
## 5. “What is added on top of stateless LLM API calls?” chart
This might be the most useful chart for your conceptual understanding.
![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,..)
flowchart LR
    A["Stateless LLM API call"] --> B["Prompt construction"]
    B --> C["Model response"]
    C --> D["Agent wrapper adds state"]
    D --> E["Conversation history / session memory"]
    D --> F["Project context / retrieved files"]
    D --> G["Tool results / observations"]
    C --> H["Tool layer"]
    H --> I["Search tools"]
    H --> J["Filesystem tools"]
    H --> K["Terminal tools"]
    H --> L["Browser tools"]
    H --> M["Git / PR tools"]
    H --> N["External APIs / MCP servers"]
    C --> O["Control loop"]
    O --> P["Plan"]
    P --> Q["Act"]
    Q --> R["Observe"]
    R --> S["Revise"]
    S --> P
    O --> T["Safety and UX layer"]
    T --> U["Permissions"]
    T --> V["Diff review"]
    T --> W["Artifacts"]
    T --> X["Human approval"]
A raw model API is just:
```text
messages in → tokens out
```
An agent product adds:
```text
state + tools + control loop + environment access + observation + safety + UI
```
That is why Antigravity and Claude Code are not merely “LLM API wrappers.” They are full agent runtimes/products around the model.
***
## 6. Very compact comparison Mermaid diagram
If you want one small chart for notes or slides, use this:
![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,..)
flowchart LR
    LLM["Stateless LLM API"]
    subgraph F["FitFindr Homework"]
        F1["Python loop"]
        F2["Session dict"]
        F3["Fixed tools"]
        F4["LLM called inside some tools"]
    end
    subgraph P["Paper LLM Agent"]
        P1["Reasoning loop"]
        P2["Tool selection"]
        P3["Observation"]
        P4["Memory/context"]
    end
    subgraph C["Antigravity / Claude Code"]
        C1["Agent runtime"]
        C2["Codebase access"]
        C3["File edits"]
        C4["Terminal/tests/git"]
        C5["Artifacts/diffs/PRs"]
        C6["Human review"]
    end
    LLM --> F
    LLM --> P
    LLM --> C
    F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P1
    C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4 --> C5 --> C6
***
## 7. Is Mermaid the right tool?
Yes, for your purpose, Mermaid is a good choice because you’re trying to understand **process structure**, not produce a polished graphic.
It is especially good for:
* comparing workflows,
* showing loops,
* showing tool calls,
* showing where the LLM sits,
* showing differences between homework code and real agent products.
For your homework writeup, I would use **one compact chart** plus a short explanation. The best one is probably the **“FitFindr homework agent flow”** chart, because it directly explains your implementation. If you want to discuss broader context, add the **“What is added on top of stateless LLM API calls?”** chart.

comments.author: Agent Answer
comments.text:
Yes — this is a great question, because **“agent” has a much older and fuzzier origin than RAG**. Short version:
> **RAG has a relatively clear naming origin: the 2020 Lewis et al. paper “Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks.” “Agent” does not have one clean LLM-era origin; it comes from older AI, robotics, control theory, distributed systems, and multi-agent systems, then got re-applied to LLM wrappers that can plan, use tools, observe results, and iterate.** [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401), [\[proceeding...neurips.cc\]](https:proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2020/file/6b493230205f780e1bc26945df7481e5-Paper.pdf), [\[cs.ox.ac.uk\]](https:www.cs.ox.ac.uk/people/michael.wooldridge/pubs/ker95/ker95-html.html), [\[open.edu\]](https:www.open.edu/openlearn/mod/oucontent/view.php?id=116249&section=2.4)
***
## 1. Where did “agent” come from originally?
The idea of an **agent** predates LLMs by decades. In classical AI, the influential Russell & Norvig framing defines AI around **agents** that perceive their environment, act, persist over time, adapt to change, and pursue goals. [\[open.edu\]](https:www.open.edu/openlearn/mod/oucontent/view.php?id=116249&section=2.4), [\[people.eec...rkeley.edu\]](https:people.eecs.berkeley.edu/~russell/aima1e.html)
A common textbook-style definition is:
> An agent is something that **perceives** its environment through sensors and **acts** on that environment through actuators. [\[en.wikipedia.org\]](https:en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intelligent_agent), [\[linkedin.com\]](https:www.linkedin.com/pulse/norvigs-agent-definition-matt-rickard-msmcf)
In software, “sensors” and “actuators” do not have to be physical cameras and robot arms. A software agent can “perceive” by reading files, receiving API results, seeing test output, or observing browser state, and it can “act” by editing files, calling APIs, sending messages, running commands, or updating a database. [\[en.wikipedia.org\]](https:en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intelligent_agent), [\[cs.ox.ac.uk\]](https:www.cs.ox.ac.uk/people/michael.wooldridge/pubs/ker95/ker95-html.html)
The 1995 Wooldridge & Jennings paper **“Intelligent Agents: Theory and Practice”** is one of the important pre-LLM references because it explicitly discusses agent theory, agent architectures, and agent languages as separate concerns, showing that “agent” was already a serious AI/software-engineering concept long before ChatGPT. [\[cs.cmu.edu\]](https:www.cs.cmu.edu/~motionplanning/papers/sbp_papers/integrated1/woodridge_intelligent_agents.pdf), [\[cs.ox.ac.uk\]](https:www.cs.ox.ac.uk/people/michael.wooldridge/pubs/ker95/ker95-html.html)
So the origin is not “someone started calling LLMs agents.” The older idea was:
```text
Agent = entity/program that observes environment + chooses actions + pursues goals
```
Then LLM researchers and developers later asked:
```text
Can an LLM be used as the reasoning/planning component inside such an agent?
```
***
## 2. When did LLMs start being called agents?
The LLM-specific usage became prominent around **2022–2023**, especially when people started wrapping LLMs in loops that let them reason, call tools, observe results, and continue. ReAct, submitted in October 2022, explicitly combined **reasoning traces** and **task-specific actions**, letting LLMs interface with external sources such as knowledge bases or environments. [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629), [\[huggingface.co\]](https:huggingface.co/papers/2210.03629)
Toolformer, submitted in February 2023, pushed a related idea: train a language model to decide **which APIs to call, when to call them, what arguments to pass, and how to incorporate results**, using tools such as calculators, search engines, translation systems, calendars, and Q\&A systems. [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2302.04761), [\[proceeding...neurips.cc\]](https:proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/hash/d842425e4bf79ba039352da0f658a906-Abstract-Conference.html)
Then projects like **AutoGPT** and **BabyAGI** popularized the public image of “LLM agents” in 2023 by putting GPT models inside autonomous-looking task loops that could break goals into subtasks, use tools, browse, manage files, execute code, and iterate. [\[en.wikipedia.org\]](https:en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AutoGPT), [\[ikangai.com\]](https:www.ikangai.com/babyagi-and-autogpt-the-rise-of-autonomous-ai-agents/)
So a rough timeline is:
```text
Pre-LLM AI:
  Agent = perceive + act + pursue goals
1990s:
  Intelligent agents / multi-agent systems become a mature AI/software topic
2020:
  RAG is named clearly in the Lewis et al. retrieval-augmented generation paper
2022:
  ReAct shows LLMs interleaving reasoning and action/tool use
2023:
  Toolformer, AutoGPT, BabyAGI, LangChain-style agents make “LLM agent” mainstream
```
This means **LLMs did not invent the agent concept**; LLMs became a new kind of controller or planner inside an older agent architecture. [\[open.edu\]](https:www.open.edu/openlearn/mod/oucontent/view.php?id=116249&section=2.4), [\[cs.ox.ac.uk\]](https:www.cs.ox.ac.uk/people/michael.wooldridge/pubs/ker95/ker95-html.html), [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629), [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2302.04761)
***
## 3. What qualifies as an “agent”?
There is no single universally enforced definition today, but a useful practical checklist is:
### Minimum weak definition
A system is agent-like if it has:
1. **A goal or task** — it is trying to accomplish something, not just generate unrelated text. [\[open.edu\]](https:www.open.edu/openlearn/mod/oucontent/view.php?id=116249&section=2.4), [\[cs.ox.ac.uk\]](https:www.cs.ox.ac.uk/people/michael.wooldridge/pubs/ker95/ker95-html.html)
2. **Perception / input from an environment** — it can observe something: user messages, files, web pages, database results, tool outputs, test failures, etc. [\[en.wikipedia.org\]](https:en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intelligent_agent), [\[open.edu\]](https:www.open.edu/openlearn/mod/oucontent/view.php?id=116249&section=2.4)
3. **Actions** — it can do something beyond answer text, such as call a tool, run code, search, edit files, or update state. [\[en.wikipedia.org\]](https:en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intelligent_agent), [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2302.04761)
4. **A control loop** — it can decide what to do next based on observations, often repeating `plan → act → observe → revise`. [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629), [\[agent-patt...thedocs.io\]](https:agent-patterns.readthedocs.io/en/stable/patterns/react.html)
### Stronger modern LLM-agent definition
A stronger LLM agent usually has:
1. **Planning** — it can decompose a goal into steps or subtasks. [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629), [\[en.wikipedia.org\]](https:en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AutoGPT)
2. **Tool use** — it can choose and call external tools/APIs, not only respond from model weights. [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2302.04761), [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629)
3. **Memory or state** — the application maintains conversation history, scratchpad state, task lists, retrieved documents, or long-term memory, because LLMs themselves are not inherently persistent state machines. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/PromptEngineering.tex.md), [\[ikangai.com\]](https:www.ikangai.com/babyagi-and-autogpt-the-rise-of-autonomous-ai-agents/)
4. **Observation-based iteration** — it can inspect outputs/errors/results and change its next action. [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629), [\[agent-patt...thedocs.io\]](https:agent-patterns.readthedocs.io/en/stable/patterns/react.html)
5. **Some autonomy** — it can perform multiple steps without requiring the human to manually specify every next API call or command. [\[en.wikipedia.org\]](https:en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AutoGPT), [\[ikangai.com\]](https:www.ikangai.com/babyagi-and-autogpt-the-rise-of-autonomous-ai-agents/)
A plain one-shot LLM call like:
```text
prompt → answer
```
is usually **not** an agent in the strong sense, because it lacks persistent state, environment action, tool use, and iterative decision-making. The attached paper also emphasizes that LLMs themselves have **transient state**, so applications must add session/context management externally. [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/PromptEngineering.tex.md)
***
## 4. Is the LLM itself the agent?
Usually, no. More precisely:
> **The LLM is often the reasoning/policy component inside the agent, but the agent is the whole system: LLM + state + tools + environment + control loop + permissions/guardrails.** [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629), [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2302.04761), [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/PromptEngineering.tex.md)
For example, in your FitFindr homework:
```text
Python loop + session dict + tools + LLM calls = simple agent system
```
The LLM calls inside `suggest_outfit()` or `create_fit_card()` are not by themselves the full agent; the surrounding Python loop is doing orchestration. Your project files describe `agent.py` as the planning loop that orchestrates tools and passes state via a `session` dictionary. [\[antigravity.google\]](https:antigravity.google/)
In a more advanced coding product:
```text
LLM + project context + file reader + editor + terminal + test runner + git + review UI = coding agent
```
This is why products like Claude Code or Antigravity feel more “agentic” than a chat API: they give the model an environment to observe and tools to act with. Claude Code is described as an agentic coding tool that lives in your terminal, understands your codebase, executes routine tasks, explains code, and handles git workflows.  Antigravity 2.0 is described as a platform for orchestrating multiple autonomous agents, with subagents, scheduled tasks, artifacts, projects, skills, MCPs, and hooks. [\[mindtwo.com\]](https:www.mindtwo.com/blog/claude-code-ai-coding-assistants) [\[antigravityide.help\]](https:www.antigravityide.help/)
***
## 5. Does “agent” have as clear an origin as RAG?
No — **RAG has a clearer origin than “agent.”**
The term **Retrieval-Augmented Generation** is strongly associated with the 2020 Lewis et al. paper, which explicitly introduced RAG models combining a pre-trained seq2seq model as parametric memory with a dense vector index of Wikipedia as non-parametric memory. [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401), [\[proceeding...neurips.cc\]](https:proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2020/file/6b493230205f780e1bc26945df7481e5-Paper.pdf)
By contrast, **agent** is a broad term with roots in classical AI, cybernetics, robotics, economics, distributed computing, and multi-agent systems, and it was already formalized in AI textbooks and agent theory papers before LLMs existed. [\[en.wikipedia.org\]](https:en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intelligent_agent), [\[open.edu\]](https:www.open.edu/openlearn/mod/oucontent/view.php?id=116249&section=2.4), [\[cs.ox.ac.uk\]](https:www.cs.ox.ac.uk/people/michael.wooldridge/pubs/ker95/ker95-html.html)
So you can think of it like this:
| Term                      | Origin clarity | Why                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| ------------------------- | -------------: | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **RAG**                   |           High | The name is tied to a specific 2020 paper and architecture: retrieval + generation. [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401), [\[proceeding...neurips.cc\]](https:proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2020/file/6b493230205f780e1bc26945df7481e5-Paper.pdf)                                                                                                                                             |
| **LLM agent**             |         Medium | Became popular through 2022–2023 LLM tool-use and autonomous-agent work like ReAct, Toolformer, AutoGPT, and BabyAGI. [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629), [\[arxiv.org\]](https:arxiv.org/abs/2302.04761), [\[en.wikipedia.org\]](https:en.wikipedia.org/wiki/AutoGPT), [\[ikangai.com\]](https:www.ikangai.com/babyagi-and-autogpt-the-rise-of-autonomous-ai-agents/) |
| **Agent in AI generally** |  Low-to-medium | It is an older broad concept, formalized across many AI traditions rather than coined by one single modern paper. [\[open.edu\]](https:www.open.edu/openlearn/mod/oucontent/view.php?id=116249&section=2.4), [\[cs.ox.ac.uk\]](https:www.cs.ox.ac.uk/people/michael.wooldridge/pubs/ker95/ker95-html.html)                                                                                 |
***
## 6. My practical definition for your homework
For your homework, I would define an agent this way:
> An **AI agent** is an application-level system that uses an LLM and/or other logic to pursue a goal by maintaining state, choosing actions, using tools, observing results, and iterating until it returns an answer or completes a task.
Then you can classify systems:
```text
One LLM API call:
  Not really an agent.
LLM call inside a fixed Python workflow:
  Weak/simple agent or agent-like workflow.
LLM chooses tools in a plan-act-observe loop:
  Stronger LLM agent.
LLM coding product with filesystem, terminal, git, tests, browser, memory, permissions:
  Production agentic system.
```
For FitFindr specifically, I’d call it a **simple manually orchestrated agent**, not a fully autonomous LLM agent. It qualifies because it has a user goal, a session state, and tool calls, but it is weakly agentic because the Python code mostly fixes the sequence of actions instead of letting the LLM dynamically choose tools. [\[antigravity.google\]](https:antigravity.google/), [\[unmm-my.sh...epoint.com\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/PromptEngineering.tex.md)

comments.author: Agent Answer
comments.text:
Yes — based on the lecture slide definition, **your homework is agent-like, but probably not a full ReAct-style agent unless your implementation lets the model choose tools dynamically and reason again after each tool result**.
The lecture describes a multi-tool AI agent as a system where the **model reasons, requests a tool call, your code executes the tool, your code appends the tool result, and then the model reasons again with the observation**. The slide calls this the **ReAct Loop: Reason → Act → Observe → Repeat**, where “the model sees the full history of observations” and each result shapes what it decides next. [\[Su26 AI201...gent \[S1c\] | Txt\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/Su26%20AI201%20Applications%20of%20AI%20Engineering%20-%20Module%201%20Lesson%202%20-%20Multi-Tool%20AI%20Agent%20%5BS1c%5D.txt)
Your FitFindr homework has **some of these pieces**, but depending on your actual `agent.py`, it may be more of a **manual orchestration loop** than a true model-driven ReAct loop.
***
## 1. What the lecture means by “agent”
The lecture’s agent model is roughly:
```text
User asks a question
↓
Model reasons about what it needs
↓
Model returns a structured tool call
↓
Your code executes the function
↓
Your code appends the tool result
↓
Model sees the result
↓
Model reasons again
↓
Model either calls another tool or gives final answer
```
The slides are very explicit on this point:
> “The model doesn’t call your function. It tells you what to call. Your agent code receives the tool call, runs the function, and hands the result back to the model.” [\[Su26 AI201...gent \[S1c\] | Txt\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/Su26%20AI201%20Applications%20of%20AI%20Engineering%20-%20Module%201%20Lesson%202%20-%20Multi-Tool%20AI%20Agent%20%5BS1c%5D.txt)
So in the lecture, an agent is not just:
```text
Python calls LLM once
```
It is more like:
```text
LLM decides → code executes → LLM observes → LLM decides again
```
The lecture demo architecture also includes four layers:
```text
TOOL REGISTRY
↓
MODEL + ReAct
Reason → Act → Observe → Repeat
↓
STATE TRACKER
↓
FINAL RESPONSE
```
The pseudocode uses a `while not done` loop, sends `messages` and `TOOLS` to the model, checks for `response.tool_calls`, executes the selected function from `TOOL_REGISTRY`, appends the result, and continues until the model gives a final answer or a max-iteration guard stops it. [\[Su26 AI201...gent \[S1c\] | Txt\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/Su26%20AI201%20Applications%20of%20AI%20Engineering%20-%20Module%201%20Lesson%202%20-%20Multi-Tool%20AI%20Agent%20%5BS1c%5D.txt)
***
## 2. Does your homework let the LLM reason?
**Partly yes, but probably only inside certain tools.**
In the FitFindr project bundle, the three required tools are:
```text
search_listings(description, size, max_price)
suggest_outfit(new_item, wardrobe)
create_fit_card(outfit, new_item)
```
The project file describes these as standalone Python functions, and `agent.py` as the planning loop that orchestrates them using a session dict. [\[linkedin.com\]](https:www.linkedin.com/pulse/norvigs-agent-definition-matt-rickard-msmcf)
If `suggest_outfit()` calls Groq/LLM, then the LLM reasons about styling:
```text
Given this thrifted item and this wardrobe, suggest outfits.
```
If `create_fit_card()` calls Groq/LLM, then the LLM reasons about caption/style:
```text
Given this outfit and item, generate a fit card caption.
```
But that is different from the lecture’s stronger meaning, where the **LLM reasons about which tool to call next**.
So:
```text
LLM reasons about outfit text? Yes.
LLM reasons about tool selection/order? Maybe not, likely no if Python hardcodes the sequence.
```
***
## 3. Does your homework let the LLM call tools?
**Probably no, not in the lecture’s strict sense.**
In the lecture, “tool calling” means the model returns a structured response like:
```json
{
  "id": "call_abc123",
  "name": "search_upcoming_shows",
  "arguments": {
    "artist_name": "Chappell Roan"
  }
}
```
Then your code reads that structured tool call and executes the function. [\[Su26 AI201...gent \[S1c\] | Txt\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/Su26%20AI201%20Applications%20of%20AI%20Engineering%20-%20Module%201%20Lesson%202%20-%20Multi-Tool%20AI%20Agent%20%5BS1c%5D.txt)
Your FitFindr homework, based on the project discussion and core bundle, sounds more like:
```python
listings = search_listings(description, size, max_price)
outfit = suggest_outfit(listing, wardrobe)
fit_card = create_fit_card(outfit, listing)
```
That means **Python is calling the tools directly**, not the LLM choosing a tool through structured tool calling. The previous project discussion also said the homework does **not** require a complex JSON-based function-calling protocol and that the tools are standard Python functions called by `agent.py`. [\[Su26 AI201...gent \[S1c\] | Txt\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/Su26%20AI201%20Applications%20of%20AI%20Engineering%20-%20Module%201%20Lesson%202%20-%20Multi-Tool%20AI%20Agent%20%5BS1c%5D.txt)
So the precise answer is:
> Your homework uses tools, but the LLM may not be “calling” them. The Python agent loop is calling them.
That is still acceptable for the homework if the assignment expects a simpler manually orchestrated agent.
***
## 4. Does your homework let the LLM observe results?
**Partly yes, if tool outputs are passed into later LLM prompts.**
For example, suppose your Python code does this:
```python
listings = search_listings(description, size, max_price)
selected_item = listings[0]
outfit = suggest_outfit(selected_item, wardrobe)
```
Then the LLM inside `suggest_outfit()` is “observing” the selected listing because your code includes that listing in the prompt.
But it is not observing in the full lecture/ReAct sense unless the conversation history includes:
```text
Assistant requested tool call
Tool returned result
Assistant reasons again
```
The lecture emphasizes that your code appends the assistant tool-call message and then appends a tool-result message referencing the same ID, so the model can see its own request and the result together. [\[Su26 AI201...gent \[S1c\] | Txt\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/Su26%20AI201%20Applications%20of%20AI%20Engineering%20-%20Module%201%20Lesson%202%20-%20Multi-Tool%20AI%20Agent%20%5BS1c%5D.txt)
Your homework probably does simpler observation:
```text
Python gets search result
Python inserts search result into next LLM prompt
LLM generates outfit suggestion
```
That is observation, but **application-mediated observation**, not full structured ReAct observation.
***
## 5. Comparison: lecture agent vs your FitFindr homework
```text
Lecture ReAct Agent
-------------------
Model decides which tool to call.
Code executes the chosen tool.
Code appends tool result to messages.
Model sees result and reasons again.
Loop continues until final answer.
FitFindr Homework Agent
-----------------------
Python parses user query.
Python calls search_listings().
Python chooses/uses result.
Python calls LLM-backed suggest_outfit().
Python calls LLM-backed create_fit_card().
Session dict stores outputs.
```
The important difference:
```text
Lecture: model controls tool choice.
FitFindr: Python likely controls tool order.
```
The lecture even warns against a fixed predefined sequence in some cases. For the empty-result question, the correct answer is that the agent should “reason about the empty result and decide whether to try a different approach or inform the user,” rather than “skip to the next tool in its predefined sequence.” [\[Su26 AI201...gent \[S1c\] | Txt\]](https:unmm-my.sharepoint.com/personal/tallpik3_unm_edu/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/Su26%20AI201%20Applications%20of%20AI%20Engineering%20-%20Module%201%20Lesson%202%20-%20Multi-Tool%20AI%20Agent%20%5BS1c%5D.txt)
Your stretch fallback plan moves closer to this idea, because when `search_listings()` returns `[]`, the agent can call `retry_search_with_fallback()` instead of immediately failing. But if Python hardcodes that fallback, it is still a manual control loop rather than the LLM dynamically deciding the next tool. [\[en.wikipedia.org\]](https:en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intelligent_agent)
***
## 6. So what should you say for the homework?
I would describe your FitFindr agent like this:
> FitFindr implements a simplified multi-tool agent pattern. It has multiple tools, a session state object, and an orchestration loop that passes outputs from one step into the next. Unlike the full ReAct pattern from lecture, the model does not necessarily choose tools through structured tool calls; instead, the Python `run_agent()` loop calls the tools in a mostly fixed sequence. The LLM is used inside specific tools, such as outfit suggestion and fit-card generation, while the Python code handles planning, state, and tool orchestration.
That wording is accurate and honest.
***
## 7. Mermaid chart: lecture ReAct vs FitFindr
Here is the plain-text Mermaid version:
***
## 8. Bottom line
Your homework does have:
* **tools**,
* **state tracking** through a session dict,
* **orchestration**,
* **LLM reasoning inside some tools**,
* **passing results from one step to another**.
But compared with the lecture’s ReAct agent, your homework may **not** have:
* model-selected tool calls,
* structured `tool_call_id` messages,
* the model observing its own tool-call result in the conversation history,
* dynamic loop behavior where the model decides whether to call another tool.
So the best label is:
> **FitFindr is a simplified, manually orchestrated multi-tool AI agent. It demonstrates the same orchestration challenges as the lecture agent, but it is not necessarily a full ReAct tool-calling agent unless you implement model-driven tool selection and repeated model observation.**

This is mixed:
```text
Tool call itself: Level 2, because Python calls the tool.
LLM reasoning inside the tool: Yes, but not tool-selection reasoning.
```
So this line does let the LLM reason about styling, but it does not mean the LLM chose to call `suggest_outfit()`.
A good way to say it:
> The LLM reasons inside the `suggest_outfit` tool, but the Python agent decides when to invoke that tool.
