# Summary mapping
***
# Where FitFindr does and does not match the lecture ReAct loop
## Lecture ReAct loop
```text
Reason:
  Model decides what it needs.
Act:
  Model returns structured tool call.
Observe:
  Code executes tool and appends result.
Repeat:
  Model reasons again with the result.
```
The lecture describes this exact cycle as `Reason → Act → Observe → Repeat`. [\[npmjs.com\]](https:www.npmjs.com/package/@mermaid-js/layout-elk)
## FitFindr loop
```text
Reason:
  Mostly Python decides the workflow.
  LLM reasons only inside generation tools.
Act:
  Python calls tools directly.
Observe:
  Python receives tool outputs.
  LLM observes only the data Python includes in later prompts.
Repeat:
  Usually fixed sequence, not open-ended model-driven loop.
```
So:
| Capability                                       |    FitFindr? | Explanation                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| ------------------------------------------------ | -----------: | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Multiple tools                                   |          Yes | `search_listings`, `suggest_outfit`, and `create_fit_card` are standalone tools. [\[github.com\]](https:github.com/zauberzeug/nicegui/discussions/5552)                                                                              |
| State tracking                                   |          Yes | `agent.py` passes state through a session dict. [\[github.com\]](https:github.com/zauberzeug/nicegui/discussions/5552)                                                                                                               |
| LLM reasoning                                    |       Partly | LLM likely reasons inside `suggest_outfit` and `create_fit_card`, not necessarily about tool choice. [\[github.com\]](https:github.com/zauberzeug/nicegui/discussions/5552)                                                          |
| Model-selected tool calls                        |  Probably no | Prior project discussion says the code simply calls Python functions and does not implement a complex JSON function-calling protocol. [\[npmjs.com\]](https:www.npmjs.com/package/@mermaid-js/layout-elk)                            |
| Model observes tool result and chooses next tool |  Probably no | In FitFindr, Python likely observes search results and decides the next step. The lecture’s full version would append tool results for the model to reason over. [\[npmjs.com\]](https:www.npmjs.com/package/@mermaid-js/layout-elk) |
| ReAct loop                                       | Weak/partial | It has action and observation, but the loop is mostly Python-driven rather than model-driven. [\[npmjs.com\]](https:www.npmjs.com/package/@mermaid-js/layout-elk)                                                                    |
| MCP                                              |           No | The project discussion says it is not directly related to MCP and uses a simpler Python planning loop. [\[npmjs.com\]](https:www.npmjs.com/package/@mermaid-js/layout-elk)                                                           |
***
# How to argue whether it is a “real agent”
I would phrase it like this in your homework:
> FitFindr is a simplified multi-tool AI agent. It has a user goal, multiple tools, state tracking through a session dictionary, and an orchestration loop that passes outputs from one tool into the next. However, it is not a full ReAct-style model-driven agent as described in lecture, because the LLM does not appear to select tools through structured tool calls or reason again after each tool result. Instead, the Python `run_agent()` function manually controls the sequence: search listings, suggest outfit, then create fit card. Therefore, FitFindr is best described as a manually orchestrated or weakly agentic system, rather than a fully autonomous LLM agent.
That is the clearest and safest answer.
***
# If you want to make FitFindr more “real agent” later
You would change this:
```python
listings = search_listings(description, size, max_price)
outfit = suggest_outfit(listings[0], wardrobe)
fit_card = create_fit_card(outfit, listings[0])
```
into something closer to the lecture:
```python
while not done:
    response = model.chat(
        messages=messages,
        tools=TOOLS
    )
    if response.tool_calls:
        for call in response.tool_calls:
            result = TOOL_REGISTRY**call.arguments
            messages.append(response.message)
            messages.append({
                "role": "tool",
                "tool_call_id": call.id,
                "content": result
            })
    else:
        done = True
```
The lecture’s demo pseudocode uses this same pattern: call the model with messages and tools, check `response.tool_calls`, execute from a tool registry, append the tool result, and stop when the model gives a final answer or a max-iteration guard triggers. [\[npmjs.com\]](https:www.npmjs.com/package/@mermaid-js/layout-elk)
Then FitFindr would become:
```text
Level 1: native model-driven tool-calling agent
```
instead of:
```text
Level 2: manually orchestrated multi-tool agent
```
Final one-sentence version:
> **Your FitFindr homework is a real agent in the classroom/simplified sense, but not a full ReAct/tool-calling agent in the lecture’s strict sense unless the LLM itself chooses tools, receives tool observations, and decides the next action in a loop.**
